policy_statement: Legally protect an additional 20,000 acres of carbon pools on natural lands and manage all new and existing natural areas (~70,000 acres total) for resilience by 2030.
mechanism_description: Protecting and conserving natural lands preserves ecosystem carbon stocks and associated ecological functions, avoiding emissions from land conversion and supporting resilience.
primary_causal_pathway: Land protection (avoid conversion/degradation) → preserved ecosystem carbon stocks + avoided land-use-change emissions → ↓ net GHG emissions / maintained carbon storage
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Governments designate, acquire, or otherwise legally protect natural lands (and manage them to prevent degradation), limiting development and disruptive land uses. (2) Process: Avoided vegetation/soil disturbance reduces carbon oxidation, biomass loss, and soil carbon release; intact ecosystems continue to retain carbon and maintain ecological function. (3) Outcome: Lower net GHG emissions relative to a conversion baseline and preservation of existing carbon pools (reduced atmospheric forcing over time).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoiding GHG emissions by preventing land conversion/degradation and preserving existing ecosystem carbon stocks.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: preventing land-use change preserves carbon pools and avoids emissions from clearing/draining/disturbing natural areas.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while “green/blue space” protection can reduce heat and flooding impacts, those adaptation benefits are not the single dominant climate effect across all instances; the mechanism as defined explicitly targets ecosystem/carbon preservation and avoidance of conversion emissions.  
3) Inherent causal chain for each secondary: None—heat mitigation, flood buffering, biodiversity, and water quality benefits are common but not physically guaranteed for every protected parcel (they depend on ecosystem type, location, management, and hazard context).  
4) Edge cases applied: “Manage for resilience” can include adaptation actions, but the mechanism remains land protection for carbon preservation; adaptation outcomes vary and therefore are not inherent secondaries.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: any policy that protects natural land to preserve ecosystems/carbon has a direct mitigation effect through avoided land-use-change emissions and maintained carbon stocks, regardless of city or ecosystem type.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some protected “green and blue spaces” are pursued primarily for adaptation/recreation; if a policy protects highly managed/low-carbon areas (e.g., ornamental parks) or leads to substantial new irrigation/inputs, mitigation benefits may be smaller, but the canonical mechanism assumes meaningful ecosystem/carbon preservation via avoided conversion/degradation.
additional_secondary_evidence: Flood risk management: Austin faces flash flooding, and legally protecting and managing natural lands for resilience directly reduces exposure/sensitivity because conserved/healthy ecosystems increase infiltration and floodwater storage and can reduce runoff intensity and downstream flood peaks.
key_indicators: “legally protect”, “additional 20,000 acres”, “carbon pools on natural lands”, “manage all new and existing natural areas”, “focusing on resilience”, “By 2030”
co_benefits: Heat risk reduction (urban cooling via expanded natural cover); Drought/water supply resilience (soil moisture retention/groundwater recharge support); Wildfire risk reduction potential (if resilience management includes fuels/ecosystem management) — not explicit in text.
instance_edge_case_notes: “Resilience” is not tied to a specific hazard in the text; flood-risk secondary is inferred from the mechanism of conserving/managing natural lands (green infrastructure function). If “resilience” management were clarified to focus on wildfire fuels, drought, or heat, the secondary category might shift accordingly.
